revision.text:
[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]]{{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version. </ref> are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=05 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. For this reason, this kind of shrine is now sometimes called {{nihongo|''setsumassha''|摂末社}}. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumassha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'')<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|language=English|accessdate=05 December 2009}}</ref>
From the middle ages onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref>thorough a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumassha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aratama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''shakaku''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]{{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha'' which owes its name to the fact that, unlike the other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at the entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present.
==References== <references/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> Image:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large sessha Image:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] Image:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' </gallery> {{Japan-art-stub}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[ja:摂末社]]

[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]]{{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version. </ref> are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=05 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. For this reason, this kind of shrine is now sometimes called {{nihongo|''setsumassha''|摂末社}}. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumassha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'')<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|language=English|accessdate=05 December 2009}}</ref>
From the middle ages onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref>thorough a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumassha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aratama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''shakaku''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]{{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha'' which owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at the entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present.

During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|language=English|accessdate=05 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History_of_Japan#Feudal_Japan_.2812th_-_19th_century.29|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref>through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumassha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]{{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha'' which owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at the entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present.

From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumassha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''tsumairi''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or {{nihongo|''hirairi''|平入}} or, more frequently, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''nagare-zukuri'' or ''kasuga-zukuri'' styles.

<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Shinto architecture}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[ja:摂末社]]

[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}) are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. For this reason, this kind of shrine is now sometimes called {{nihongo|''setsumatsusha''|摂末社}}.<ref group="note">The term ''setsumassha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref> They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History_of_Japan#Feudal_Japan_.2812th_-_19th_century.29|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''tsumairi''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or {{nihongo|''hirairi''|平入}} or, more frequently, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''nagare-zukuri'' or ''kasuga-zukuri'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Shinto architecture}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[ja:摂末社]]

Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''nagare-zukuri'' or ''kasuga-zukuri'' styles.

[[ja:摂末社]]

<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref>[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History_of_Japan#Feudal_Japan_.2812th_-_19th_century.29|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''tsumairi''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or {{nihongo|''hirairi''|平入}} or, more frequently, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''nagare-zukuri'' or ''kasuga-zukuri'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Shinto architecture}}

[[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[ken]]'' in size. During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History_of_Japan#Feudal_Japan_.2812th_-_19th_century.29|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.

[[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref>[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref>[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have different meanings, but must be today considered synonyms. They can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

revision.comment: Deleted redirect here and moved it to Massha, which is now a Disambig
revision.text:
[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|branch shrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines having a deep historical relationship with a more important shrine or with the ''[[kami]]'' it enshrines, and fall under that shrine's jurisdiction.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref> The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A massha at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[fr:Setsumatsusha]] [[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at Iwa Shrine, [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[fr:Setsumatsusha]] [[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]]

[[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri].</ref>

From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.

However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 05 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm Misedana-zukuri]. Retrieved 5 December 2009.</ref> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

revision.comment: fix ref error (ref name used more than once with different info. within parameters) + bare url
revision.text:
{{Use dmy dates|date=April 2013}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''Sessha'''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''massha'''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|''eda-miya''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''setsumatsusha'''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|accessdate=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates [[recorded history|written history]]. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined <ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''.</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in Ōsaka]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]]

Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

Japan|language=Japanese|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|accessdate=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates [[recorded history|written history]]. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01JPG.JPG|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Architecture of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]]

The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|accessdate=15 March 2016}}</ref>

However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

revision.text:
{{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=April 2013}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.is/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.is/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates [[recorded history|written history]]. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Architecture of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]

Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.

Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

revision.comment: Adding local [[Wikipedia:Short description|short description]]: "Miniature Japanese shrines", overriding Wikidata description "Shrines"
revision.text:
{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=April 2013}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha - Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates [[recorded history|written history]]. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]

Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/wip/HFCJP</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
