revision.comment: Tagging pages with bare URLs for citation, prior to follow up with tools (via [[WP:JWB]])
revision.text:
{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates [[recorded history|written history]]. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/wip/HFCJP {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]]

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia38]] [[en:Setsumatsusha]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Setsumatsusha]]

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

[[category:kk]][[category:Shinto architecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[category:kk]][[category:Setsumatsusha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Setsumatsusha]]

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[category:kk]][[category:Shinto architecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[category:kk]][[category:Setsumatsusha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Setsumatsusha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[en:Setsumatsusha]]

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />

They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! {{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

From the [[Meiji period]] to the [[Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[Sankō Shrine]] in [[Ōsaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[Heian period]], [[Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
