{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1071160]] Wikidata name: setsumatsusha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>

==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Setsumatsusha]][[es:Setsumatsusha]][[fr:Setsumatsusha]][[ja:摂末社]][[zh:攝末社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1071160]] Wikidata name: setsumatsusha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[:en:Sankō Shrine|Sankō Shrine]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Setsumatsusha]][[es:Setsumatsusha]][[fr:Setsumatsusha]][[ja:摂末社]][[zh:攝末社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1071160]] Wikidata name: setsumatsusha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[:en:Sankō Shrine|Sankō Shrine]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Setsumatsusha]][[es:Setsumatsusha]][[fr:Setsumatsusha]][[ja:摂末社]][[zh:攝末社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1071160]] Wikidata name: setsumatsusha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[:en:Sankō Shrine|Sankō Shrine]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[:en:Sankō Shrine|Sankō Shrine]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at [[:en:Sankō Shrine|Sankō Shrine]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at {{ill|Sankō Shrine|en|Sankō Shrine|WD=Q3472342|lt=Sankō Shrine}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>

''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at {{ill|Sankō Shrine|en|Sankō Shrine|WD=Q3472342|lt=Sankō Shrine}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at {{ill|Sankō Shrine|en|Sankō Shrine|WD=Q3472342|lt=Sankō Shrine}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/> {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
[[en:Setsumatsusha]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Setsumatsusha]] [[fr:Setsumatsusha]] [[ja:摂末社]] [[zh:攝末社]] {{wikidata link|Q1071160}} [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{{Short description|Miniature Japanese shrines}} {{italic title}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Iwa jinja 11.JPG|thumb|275 px|A row of ''sessha'' at [[Iwa Shrine]], {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Sessha'''''|末社|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine}} and {{nihongo|'''''massha'''''|末社|lt=Massha|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|undershrine}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''eda-miya'''''|枝宮|branch shrines}}<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version.</ref> (collectively known as {{nihongo|'''''setsumatsusha'''''|摂末社|}}<ref name="sgk">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha, massha|last=Mure|first=Jin|publisher=Shokagukan|language=ja|access-date=9 March 2010}}</ref><ref group="note">The term ''setsumatsusha'' is the combination of the two terms ''sessha'' and ''massha''.</ref>) are small or miniature shrines entrusted to the care of a larger shrine, generally due to some deep connection with the enshrined ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121219003453/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%91%82%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BB%E6%9C%AB%E7%A4%BE/|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 December 2012|title=Sessha – Massha|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|language=ja|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
The two terms used to have legally different meanings, but are today [[:en:Synonyms|Synonyms]]. ''Setsumatsusha'' can lie either {{nihongo|inside|境内摂末社|keidai setsumatsusha}} or {{nihongo|outside|境外摂末社|keigai setsumassha}} the main shrine's premises. ''Setsumatsusha'' are usually 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'' in size. They can however be as small as [[:en:Beehives|Beehives]] or relatively large and have 1x2, 1x3 or even, in one case, 1x7 bays.<ref name="jami">{{cite web|title=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/misedanazukuri.htm|website=aisf.or.jp|access-date=15 March 2016}}</ref>
==History== The practice of building ''sessha'' and ''massha'' shrines within a ''jinja'' predates written history. The earliest ''setsumatsusha'' usually had some strong connection to the history of the area or the family of the enshrined ''kami''.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] used to make a distinction between the two types based on whether a shrine belonged to the [[Engishiki]] ''Jinmyōchō'' list (''sessha'') or to the ''Enryaku gishikichō'' list (''massha'').<ref name="Eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=266|title=Sessha, Massha|last=Mori |first=Mizue|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=5 December 2009}}</ref>
From the [[History of Japan#Feudal Japan (1185-1603)|Japanese Middle Ages]] onwards, at other shrines popular ''kami'' like [[Hachiman]], {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} or {{nihongo|Gozu Tennō|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} were often enshrined<ref group="note">Through a process called ''[[kanjō]]''</ref> in ''setsumatsusha'', but no clear distinction between the two terms was made. From the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} to the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], a shrine dedicated to family members of a ''kami'', to the {{nihongo|violent side of a kami|荒魂|aramitama}}, or the ''kami'' of the region where the main shrine was, were to be considered ''sessha'' with a higher Rank than the rest, which were called ''massha''.<ref name="Eos"/> When the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|shakaku]]''|社格}} shrine Ranking system was abolished in 1946, legally the distinction disappeared, but both terms remained in use out of habit.
Betsugu are another kind of auxiliary shrine their relationships to the main shrine are similar to that of Massha and Sessha.<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230505011443/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9702 {{bare URL inline|date=May 2023}}</ref> The term is most notable for the extensive betsugu at [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":0" />
==Architectural style== [[File:Sanko-jinja massha.jpeg|thumb|A ''massha'' at {{ill|Sankō Shrine|en|Sankō Shrine|WD=Q3472342|lt=Sankō Shrine|1=Sankō Shrine}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}]]
Being true shrines, ''setsumatsusha'' have most features other types of shrines have, including doors and often stairs. However, the {{nihongo|''Misedana-zukuri''|見世棚造 or 店棚造|showcase style}} is a style normally used only in ''sessha'' and ''massha''. It owes its name to the fact that, unlike other shrine styles, it doesn't feature a stairway at its entrance, and the veranda is completely flat.<ref name="jami"/> Miniature stairways can however be present. They can be either {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}''|妻入}}, that is have the entrance under the gable, or, more frequently, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}''|平入}}, that is, have the entrance on the side parallel to the roof's ridge (see examples in the gallery). Apart from the lack of a staircase, such shrines belong to the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' or ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' styles.
==Architectural examples==
<gallery> File:Hinomisaki-jinja sessha.jpg|A large ''sessha'' File:Kagami-no-miya-jinja. 01.jpg|A ''massha'' at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] File:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|A row of ''massha'' File:Iwashimizu Hachimangu setummasha6.jpg|A ''hirairi'' ''sessha'' File:Aguchi-jinja setsumassha2.jpg|A ''tsumairi'' ''sessha'' </gallery>
